coredata.dc:title: Aptamer SERS and RRS determination of trace lead ions using nitrogen-doped carbon dot to catalyze the new nano-gold reaction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hui
Jiang, Caina
He, Xue
Li, Chongning
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) were prepared by microwave hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate (AC) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as precursor. It was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and infrared spectroscopy (IR). The CDN was found to catalyze the reduction of HAuCl4 to produce gold nanoparticles (AuNP), among which fructose was an effective reducing agent. Using malachite green (MG) as a molecular probe, the surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) intensity at 1617 cm−1 and the resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) intensity at 375 nm increased linearly with increasing CDN concentration, respectively. The catalytic activity of CDN is inhibited because the aptamer (Apt) can be adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst CDN. The aptamer (Apt)-Pb2+ reaction and CDN-Apt adsorbing reaction were competitive reaction. When there is Pb2+ that binds more tightly to Apt, Apt is desorbed, and the catalytic ability of CDN is restored. Accordingly, an Apt-mediated nanocatalytic amplification SERS/RRS platform for quantitative detection of lead ions was constructed. For the SERS method, the linear range was 0.5–120 nmol/L with DL of 0.11 nmol/L. For the RRS method, the Pb2+ concentration was linear in the range of 50–400 nmol/L with the RRS intensity, and the DL was 15.32 nmol/L. The analysis platform uses CDN catalyzed nanoreactions to generate AuNP products with SERS activity as a substrate, thus overcoming the shortcomings of Pb2+ without scattering activity, and realizing the possibility of SERS and RRS detection of metal ions. It was used for the determination of Pb2+ in real samples with relative standard deviations were 0.94–2.71 % and recovery was 99.00–103.70 %, respectively. In addition, the mechanism of CDN nanoenzyme heterogeneous catalysis of nano-gold reactions was discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Nanocatalysis
Aptamer
SERS
RRS
Lead ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dot CDA with AC
Preparation of carbon dot CDE with EDTA
Preparation of carbon dots CDN1, CDN2, and CDN3 with AC-EDTA
SERS spectroscopy of catalytic and analytical systems
RRS spectra of the analyzed systems
Transmission electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectra (TEM, EDS)
Catalytic properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22266013, 22264005), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA035073) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Education Department (No. 2022KY0509).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs)-catalyzed nano-gold reaction-RRS/SERS dual-mode determination of lead ions.

SERS spectra of nanocatalytic system. A: 5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + CDA. B: 5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + CDE. C: 5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + CDN1. D: 5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + CDN2. E: 5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + CDN3. F: 0.10 μmol/L Apt-Pb +11.25 μg/mL CDN +5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + Pb2+. G: 0.10 μmol/L Apt-As +11.25 μg/mL CDN +5.0 mmol/L Fuc +0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 +0.10 μmoL/L MG + AsO2 −.
RRS spectra of Pb2+-Apt-CDN-Fuc-HAuCl4 analytical system, 0.1 μmol/L Apt-Pb + 11.25 μg/mL CDN + 5 mmol/L Fuc + 0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 + Pb2+.

Transmission electron microscopy and energy spectrum of the analyzed system. A, B. AuNPs generated by the Fuc- HAuCl4 reaction; C, D. AuNPs aggregated into SERS substrates in the presence of NaCl, 0.10 μmol/L Apt-Pb + 11.25 μg/mL CDN + 5.0 mmol/L Fuc + 0.25 mmol/L HAuCl4 + 0.10 μmoL/L MG + 50 mmol/L NaCl.
Analytical characteristics of SERS and RRS for Pb2+ with Apt-CDN-Fuc- HAuCl4-MG system.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * The DL was defined as a standard deviation of 3 times and calculated according to the formula DL = 3 × Sd/N. Here, the standard deviation (Sd) was obtained by 11 parallel measurements of blank samples, and the N is the slope of the working curve.

head.ce:title: Aptamer SERS and RRS determination of trace lead ions using nitrogen-doped carbon dot to catalyze the new nano-gold reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Guiling Medical University, Guiling 541199, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Guiling Medical University, Guiling 541199, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Public Health, Guiling Medical University, Guiling 541199, China (C. Li).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) were prepared by microwave hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate (AC) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as precursor. It was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and infrared spectroscopy (IR). The CDN was found to catalyze the reduction of HAuCl4 to produce gold nanoparticles (AuNP), among which fructose was an effective reducing agent. Using malachite green (MG) as a molecular probe, the surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) intensity at 1617 cm−1 and the resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) intensity at 375 nm increased linearly with increasing CDN concentration, respectively. The catalytic activity of CDN is inhibited because the aptamer (Apt) can be adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst CDN. The aptamer (Apt)-Pb2+ reaction and CDN-Apt adsorbing reaction were competitive reaction. When there is Pb2+ that binds more tightly to Apt, Apt is desorbed, and the catalytic ability of CDN is restored. Accordingly, an Apt-mediated nanocatalytic amplification SERS/RRS platform for quantitative detection of lead ions was constructed. For the SERS method, the linear range was 0.5–120 nmol/L with DL of 0.11 nmol/L. For the RRS method, the Pb2+ concentration was linear in the range of 50–400 nmol/L with the RRS intensity, and the DL was 15.32 nmol/L. The analysis platform uses CDN catalyzed nanoreactions to generate AuNP products with SERS activity as a substrate, thus overcoming the shortcomings of Pb2+ without scattering activity, and realizing the possibility of SERS and RRS detection of metal ions. It was used for the determination of Pb2+ in real samples with relative standard deviations were 0.94–2.71 % and recovery was 99.00–103.70 %, respectively. In addition, the mechanism of CDN nanoenzyme heterogeneous catalysis of nano-gold reactions was discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have good water solubility and are easily functionalized on the surface with various organic polymers, inorganic moieties, or biological species [1,2]. They have been widely used in environmental detection, food safety and biochemical analysis [3–5]. Doping other elements to improve the CDs optical and electrical properties is practical. Nitrogen is a compatible and abundant element with carbon-based materials. Doped-nitrogen CD (CDN) can significantly improve fluorescence (FL) properties due to the band-gap shift caused by the conjugate p-field [6] and are suitable for various prospective analytical applications. For example, these highly fluorescent CDN was used for the FL quenching method to detect 0.05–30 μM Hg2+ and 0.1–70 μM ClO− [7].The detection of 2.5–25 μM nitrophenol was achieved using the inner filter effect of nitrogen and silicon co-doped carbon dots multifunctional probes [8]. Based on curcumin (Cur) FL quench effect, the detection limit (DL) was 8.71 nM Cur [9]. Using CDN probe, a 1.15 × 10−6 M Fe3+ can be determined [10]. Currently, most analytical applications of CDN are focused on FL methods.

Aptamers (Apts) are designed to mimic the binding of antibodies to specific targets by folding into complex three-dimensional shapes. While some Apts exist naturally as ligand-binding elements of ribosomes, most are synthesized in vitro and can be tailored to specific targets. The real value of Apt relative to antibodies is their simplicity. They can be designed as sensors, actuators, and other components, often at the heart of emerging technologies [11,12]. Most fluorescent labeling in immunoassays increases the background interference of primers, while nano-labeling weakens the biological activity of antibodies and affects the affinity and conformational changes between antibody-target molecules, resulting in decreased sensitivity. Apts that are single-labeled or label-free can overcome these difficulties. It is also fascinating is that “label-free” relies more on meticulous and clever design, playing with the interactions between the Apt, its complementary strand, the target, and the signal probes. This characteristic demonstrates the advantages of Apt as nucleic acids, ultimately bringing the “label-free” sensor to a broader range of targets and tiny molecules. This is not easy to achieve when using other recognition elements, such as antibodies. For example, the DL of 2.15 nM Cd2+ has been achieved using a single-labeled Apt multifunctional FL probe [13]. A label-free fluorescent Apt sensor for selective FL detection of 0.7–10 nmol/L tetracycline has also been established [14]. In addition, label-free FL quenching methods for quantitatively detecting 0.7–10 nmol/L kanamycin based on functionalized molecular beacons have recently been developed [15].

Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is a molecular spectroscopic technique based on Raman scattering and localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of nanoparticles. It has been used in numerous fields, including nanomaterials research, bioanalysis, and food detection [16–20]. Molecular spectroscopy techniques are easy to use, and their coupling such as FL/colorimetry [21,22], FL/light scattering [23,24], and FL/SERS [25,26] shows better characteristics than the separate, which overcomes the shortage of a single method with the advantages of both. Liu reported a SERS/FL bimodal nanosensor with a signal transduction mechanism based on the conformational transition of G-quadruplexes in human telomeres [27], which exhibits a SERS/FL response to the complementary chain of G-quadruplexes in human telomeres. Based on the T-Hg2+-T coordination, the sensor can be used to detect Hg2+ with a DL as low as one ppm. A new SERS and FL-based dual-mode immunoassay was designed using graphene dot labels to detect tuberculosis by a newly developed antigen mycobacterium tuberculosis (CFP-10) linear contrast sensing platform. This sandwich-based immunoassay uses a dual-mode nanoprobe to recognize the SERS signal and FL image [28]. Li et al [18] reported colorimetric/FL/SERS trimodal sensing of nitrite based on a Griess-response-modulated gold nanorod-azo-nanogold assembly. Colorimetric and FL detection was performed in solution, while SERS was performed on a solid substrate, and the DL of 0.05, 0.01, and 0.0008 μM could only be achieved by their respective methods. Our group found that graphene oxide nanoribbons have a strong catalytic effect on reducing HAuCl4 by H2O2, forming AuNPs with SERS activity [29], with Apt reaction coupling, which allows the quantitative analysis of 2–75 nmol/L Pb2+ with Victoria blue B molecular probe. Amplification by preparing nitrogen and silver-co-doped carbon dots catalyzing the reduction of HAuCl4 to AuNPs by sodium citrate amplifies both RRS and SERS signals for highly sensitive detection of 0.0033–0.067 ng/mL clenbuterol with DL of 0.68 pg/mL with the help of clenbuterol antigen–antibody reaction [30]. Potassium pyroantimonate Apt was used to adsorb on the surface of calcium-doped carbon dots, inhibiting the catalytic effect and causing a decrease in the SERS peak. While the analyte Na+ reacts with pyroantimonate to produce a stable precipitate and free CDCa, the system SERS signal is restored and enhanced. Accordingly, it was possible to detect 4–43 nmol/L Na+ with a DL of 1.9 nmol/L [31].

Lead ion is a typical heavy metal pollutant that severely threatens humans, even in small amounts. There is an increasing demand for on-site detection, but it still needs to improve its sensitivity and easy-to-use methods. The current common analytical detection methods for lead ions include inductively coupled plasma combined with optical emission spectrometry, atomic emission spectrograph, and mass spectrometry techniques [32,33], but such methods suffer from expensive operating instruments, complex and time-consuming, and low sensitivity. The SERS and RRS molecular spectral methods, which are easy to operate and low-price for later, and high sensitivity for former, have become meaningful for heavy metal ion detection. In this paper, CDs with different FL properties were synthesized by a simple one-step microwave hydrothermal method. CDs with high FL intensity and intense catalytic activity were selected as nanoenzyme for the HAuCl4-fructose (Fuc) system. Using the high selectivity of nucleic acid Apt and the modulation of the catalytic activity of CDs, the two molecular spectroscopies were used to construct two molecular spectral platforms for the rapid, sensitive, and highly selective detection of trace lead ions by SERS and RRS. Compared with the reported Pb2+ detection methods, our SERS and RRS analysis strategy has the following advantages: (1) The FL property of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the phenomenon of catalytic generation of SERS substrates are used to overcome the shortcoming of Pb2+ without scattering activity, which makes SERS, and RRS detection possible; (2) The method is simple, and without the need to construct an Apt molecular probe; (3) The binding of Apt to CDs and target molecules does not affect the SERS substrate and probe, thus not reducing the sensitivity of SERS detection.

F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), adjustable voltage 300–500 V, excitation slit, emission slit = 1, 5, 10, 20 nm; DXR II smart Raman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific. USA), laser wavelength = 633 nm, power adjustable from 0.1 to 8 mW, slit = 25, 50 µm, pinhole = 25, 50 µm adjustable, acquisition time greater than or equal to 2 s; HH-S2 electric thermostatic water bath (Jintan Dadi Automation Instrument Factory, China); SYZ-550 ultra-pure water machine (Shanghai Raymag Instrument Factory, China); SK3300B ultrasonic cleaner (Shanghai Kegu Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd., China); JUPITER-B microwave digestion instrument (Shanghai Xin Yi Microwave Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China); 16 K benchtop centrifuge (Zhuhai Black Horse Medical Instrument Co., Ltd., China); Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (Thermo Scientific Company, USA), resolution 0.2–0.3 nm, effective magnification 1 million; Spectrum Two N FT-NIR infrared Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA); and Nano-ZS90 nanometer particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK) were used.

Ammonium citrate (AC, AR), ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid (EDTA, AR), fructose (monohydrate) (Fuc, AR), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, AR), malachite green (MG, AR), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 %, AR) was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China and chlorogenic acid (HAuCl4, AR) was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China. Arsenic aptamer sequence (Apt-As): 5′ -ATG CAA ACC CTT AAG AAA GTG GTC GTC CAA AAA ACC ATT G-3′. lead aptamer sequence (Apt-Pb2+): 5′-GGT TGG TGT GGT TGG-3′ was purchased from Shanghai Bioengineering Company, Shanghai, China.
0.01 mol/L HAuCl4 solution preparation method: 1.0 g of anhydrous HAuCl4 was fixed in a 25 mL volumetric flask to obtain 4 % HAuCl4 solution (W/V, 0.1177 mol/L), and 850 μL was fixed to 10.0 mL.

100 μmol/L Apt-As solution preparation method: 100 μmol/L Apt-As was prepared by adding 247 μL of water to the centrifuge tube where Apt-As was stored and shaken well. 100 μmol/L Apt-Pb was prepared by adding 698 μL of water to the centrifuge tube where Apt-Pb was stored and shaken well.

All glassware was soaked in aqua regia before the experiment, cleaned with ultrasonic cleaner for 15 min, and rinsed with ultrapure water, finally, the vessels were dried and set aside. The reagents were all of the analytical purity grade, and the experimental water was ultrapure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot CDA with AC
ce:section.ce:para: 10.0 mL 1.0 mol/L AC was added to a conical flask, and water was added to make up to 20.0 mL. The resulting colorless and transparent solution was transferred to a Teflon microwave digestion tank. The microwave power was 400 W, and the temperature was 180 °C for 30 min. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature. A light blue solution was obtained without precipitation. The carbon quantum dot solution was dialyzed for 8 h using a dialysis bag with 3500 Da and then made up to 20.0 mL. Based on the initial amount of carbon added, the concentration of CDA was 36 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot CDE with EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: 10.0 mmol EDTA (2.92 g) powder was added to a conical flask and made up to 20.0 mL. The mixture was sonicated and heated until dissolved. The resulting colorless and transparent solution was transferred to a Teflon microwave digestion vessel and heated for 30 min at 400 W and 180 °C. A dark brown solution was obtained without precipitation. The carbon quantum dot solution was dialyzed for 8 h using a dialysis bag with 3500 Da, and the solution was made up to 20.0 mL. Based on the initial amount of carbon added, the CDE concentration was 24 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots CDN1, CDN2, and CDN3 with AC-EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: 10.0 mL 1 mol/L AC was added to a conical flask, and then 1.0 mmol EDTA (0.29 g), 2.5 mmol EDTA (0.73 g), and 10.0 mmol EDTA (2.92 g) powder were added, respectively. After they were completely dissolved, the solution was diluted to 20.0 mL with water. The AC/EDTA molar ratios were 4:1, 1:1, and 1:4, respectively, and they were labeled as CDN1, CDN2, and CDN3. The resulting colorless and transparent solutions were transferred into a Teflon microwave digestion vessel, and then heated at 400 W and 180 °C for 30 min. After the reaction, the machine was automatically cooled down to room temperature, and the resulting solution was taken out. CDN1 was a dark purple solution, CDN2 was a light yellow solution, and CDN3 was a light yellow solution, all without any precipitate. The carbon quantum dot solution was dialyzed with 3500 Da for 8 h, and then diluted to 20.0 mL for later use. Based on the initial amount of carbon added, the concentrations of CDN1, CDN2, and CDN3 were 45 mg/mL, 72 mg/mL, and 96 mg/mL, respectively.

In a 5 mL stoppered graduated test tube, 50 μL (0.45 mg/mL) CDN solution and 200 μL (1 μmol/L) Apt-Pb were added sequentially and allowed to stand for 10 min, then a certain amount of Pb2+ solution was added and allowed to stand for 5 min. A 100 μL (0.1 mol/L) Fuc, 50 μL (0.01 mol/L) HAuCl4 were added and diluted to 2.0 mL, it was reacted in water bath at 75 °C for 20 min, and was terminated by ice water cooling. After that, 200 μL (1 μmol/L) MG was added as the SERS probe and, 100 μL (1 mol/L) NaCl was added and mixed. The SERS spectra were obtained by scanning at a Raman spectrometer with laser power of 2 mW and a slit of 50 μm. The SERS peak intensity IS at the Raman shift of 1617 cm−1 was measured. No Pb2+ was added as blank, the blank value IS0 was measured, and ΔIS = IS - IS0 was calculated. For the RRS, without addition of the MG and NaCl, under the conditions of fluorescence spectrophotometer voltage 350 V, excitation slit = emission slit = 5 nm and Δλ = λem-λex = 0, the RRS spectra were obtained by synchronous scanning, and the IR values at 375 nm were measured. The blank IR0 was made without Pb2+, and ΔIR = IR- IR0 was calculated.

AuNPs are nanoparticles with SPR effect, which can generate RRS signals while acting as SERS substrates. Experimental findings show that reducing sugars such as Fuc (hexose ketone) react slowly with HAuCl4 to generate AuNPs. CDs can catalyze this reaction. However, the binding of Apt with CDs reduces the catalytic activity of CDs. The behavior of Apt adsorbed to the surface of CDs is non-selective. Instead, the binding of Apt to the target molecule is more similar to covalent binding, where single chains of Apt produce bending and the overall conformation changes [34]. When a Pb2+ is present in the system, Apt binds to the target molecule in a solid complex, releasing the free CDs. The catalytic capacity of the CDs in the system is restored. The linear increase in free CDs leads to a linear increase in the amount of generated AuNPs, which the enhancement of the RRS signal can observe. A linearly enhanced SERS signal can be obtained by introducing a Raman-active probe molecule that adsorbs onto the AuNPs substrate. The dynamic process of the analysis system involving Apt is like a switch that turns on and off the detection signal of the analysis system (Fig. 1 ). Based on the characteristics of the prepared carbon dots, we can establish a quantitative RRS-SERS dual-mode molecular spectroscopy method for the rapid and sensitive detection of Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectroscopy of catalytic and analytical systems
ce:section.ce:para: The SERS signal is related to the probe molecule MG and the substrate. MG is an organic anion probe with a large Raman scattering cross section that adsorbs uniformly on the surface of the homogeneous AuNPs colloid generated by the catalytic reaction, resulting in the SERS phenomenon. There is no SERS signal for MG when the AuNP nanosol substrate does not exist. Under the condition that the concentration of the probe molecule is determined, the peak height is enhanced with increasing CDN concentration, which is attributed to the increase of AuNPs generated by the system. Although changes in the colloidal chemical environment may cause a shift in the position of the SERS peaks, the SERS peaks for MG were observed in the substrate of AuNP nanosol. Fig. 2 A-2E show that the CDs -Fuc-HAuCl4-MG catalyzed system shows five more substantial SERS peaks at 1171 cm−1, 1217 cm−1, 1366 cm−1, 1399 cm−1, and 1617 cm−1, among which the peak at 1617 cm−1 has the highest intensity and better reproducibility and was therefore chosen for the analysis. The peak at 1617 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of the biphenylimine group in the triphenylmethyl group of dyes.

After comparing the SERS spectra of the five catalytic systems CDA, CDE, CDN1, CDN2, and CDN3, it was found that CDN1 had the best catalytic effect (The slope of the fitting curve between concentration and ΔI is the largest.) for the reaction of Fuc-HAuCl4 to produce AuNPs. The cases CDN not specifically stated later are denoted as CDN1. we chose CDN1 as the catalyst for the detection of Pb2+ (Fig. 2 F) and AsO2 − (Fig. 2 G), respectively. the SERS intensity at 1617 cm−1 for the Pb2+-Apt- CDN -Fuc- HAuCl4- MG system showed a linear relationship with the Pb2+ concentration in the range of 0–120 nmol/L The linear equation was ΔI1617cm-1 = 461.5C + 699.3 (R2 = 0.9983). The SERS intensity at 1617 cm−1 for the AsO2 − -Apt- CDN-Fuc-HAuCl4-MG system was linearly dependent on the AsO2 − concentration in the 0–80 nmol/L, and the linear equation was ΔI1617cm-1 = 206.4C + 793.7 (R2 = 0.9849). It can be seen that the SERS method has a wider linear range and higher sensitivity for the detection of Pb2+. This is presumed because the G-quadrupole binding generated by Pb2+ with Apt-Pb is more stable than the AsO2 −complex with Apt-Pb, resulting in a complete desorption of Apt from the carbon dot surface and a stronger recovery of the catalytic capacity of the carbon dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RRS spectra of the analyzed systems
Transmission electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectra (TEM, EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDN sample solution was prepared according to the experimental method. After dialysis with a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 8 h, 1 mL was taken and diluted with 1 mL of ethanol (4.5 mg/mL). Then 100 μL of the sample was dropped onto the ultra-thin carbon film of the copper mesh carrier several times until it dried. the sample morphology was recorded by transmission electron microscopy. From Fig. 4 A, it can be seen that CDN is in the shape of spherical particles with a particle size of about 4–5 nm under the surface tension of water. Under the excitation of the electron beam, there are apparent diffraction streaks between the carbon–carbon lattices. The inter-lattice spacing is about 0.14–0.15 nm (Fig. 4B), which is consistent with the C(sp2)-C(sp2) conjugate bond length of 1.45–1.46 Å. The doping of nitrogen does not significantly change this dominant structure. Fig. 4 C, D shows that CDN of similar particle size is clustered together, which is attributed to the non-covalent forces generated by lone pair electron groups such as –OH, –NH2, and –NO3, which are abundant on the CDN surface. The CDN agglomerates are loosely structured with each other, and no further covalent bonds are formed at the end of the hydrothermal reaction.

Fig. 5 A and B shows the TEMs of the reaction products of the Pb2+-Apt-CDN-Fuc-HAuCl4-MG system. The reaction of Fuc-HAuCl4 to form AuNPs was catalyzed by CDN, and many spherical AuNPs with different particle sizes from 30 to 80 nm were produced. The amount of AuNPs increased with CDN released from the system. These AuNPs aggregated in the presence of high concentrations of NaCl (Fig. 5 C, D), generating substrates with the SERS effect. The energy spectrum corresponding to Fig. 5 D shows that the central element of the dark field is Au. The shape and size of the AuNPs strongly affect the intensity of the SERS enhancement, as these factors influence the absorption and scattering efficiency. Too large AuNPs will excite multipole non-radiative high-order transitions, and Raman scattering due to only dipole transitions will lead to an overall decrease in SERS. Too small particles will lose conductivity and cannot enhance the nanoplasma electric field [35]. When the particle size of AuNPs is close to a few atoms, SPR is invalid because many electrons must be present to produce resonance. The ideal SERS substrate must have a high degree of homogeneity and high field enhancement. AuNPs generated by CDN-catalyzed Fuc-HAuCl4 have a uniform particle size in an aqueous solution and are excellent SERS substrates.

10 mL of 45 mg/mL CDN1, CDN2 were placed in glass crystalline dishes, respectively. After drying under vacuum at 0.1 Pa for 24 h using a freeze dryer, the obtained viscous solid sample was spread on a potassium bromide (KBr) rock slice. The pellet was then placed into an infrared spectrometer for scanning and obtaining the corresponding spectrum, with a KBr blank pellet used as the reference. The IR spectra showed that CDN2 (Figure S1), at 3431 cm−1 (–OH stretching vibration), should be attributed to the water peak due to the inability to dry the carbon point completely; 1633 cm−1 should be the backbone vibration of the CC bond in the benzenoid ring; and 1485 cm−1 should be the stretching vibration of the free –NO2. For CDN1 (Figure S1), a combined peak appears at 3379 cm−1, which should combine –OH stretching vibration and free –NH2, –NH. The 1669 cm−1 change indicates an increase in the benzenoid ring structure. The appearance of several stronger peaks in the region of 1500–600 cm−1 indicates an increase in ether, ester, and alcohol groups, probably due to a higher degree of oxidation on the surface of CDN1. In addition, CDN1 does not show a sharp peak similar to CDN2 at 2500 cm−1 ascribing to the telescopic vibration region of CN, indicating a decrease in unsaturated C–H stretching vibrations, which suggests a complete graphitization of CDN1.

The solution of the reaction system was prepared according to the experimental method, and its particle size was recorded with a nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer. It can be seen from the Figure S2 that under the conditions without the presence of analyte Pb2+, the system produced less nanogold, and the system nanoparticle size was small (d = 31 nm). With the increase of Pb2+ concentration, the number of nanogold produced by the system gradually increased, and the system nanoparticle size gradually increased (d = 54–77 nm) (Figure S2). The main reason for the inconsistency in particle size between laser scattering and TEM is that the former is measured in solution, while the latter needs to be dried on a copper mesh to form a solid before measurement. That is to say, the aggregation of gold nanoparticles is an important reason for the inconsistency between the two results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, the slope procedure of the relationship between SERS intensity and catalyst concentration was used to evaluate the catalytic effect of the nanocatalyst, which is accurate and reliable. Catalytic reactions are zero-order reactions, and the reaction rate is only linearly related to the amount of catalyst. The slope of the working curve corresponds to the efficiency of the catalyst. Under the experimental conditions, the prepared carbon quantum dots can catalyze the reduction of HAuCl4 by reducing sugar to produce AuNPs. In a specific concentration range, as the concentration of the catalyst CDN increased, the system generated more nanoparticles. The slopes of the SERS method for the CDN1-Fuc-HAuCl4 system were all larger than those for the rest of the carbon dots (Table S1). CDN1 is more capable of catalyzing the HAuCl4-Fuc reaction.

Fuc, glucose, furfural and glycol are very weak reducing substances due to their carbonyl and unsaturated bonds. Glucose and fructose are isomers, and Fuc is hexanone, which is more stable than glucose hexanal, and the reduction of furfural and glycol is also weaker. That is, the four reducers were difficult to reduce HAuCl4 to form AuNPs. Upon addition of nanocatalyst of CDN, the AuNP reaction were enhanced (Figure S4). The Fuc system was the stronger catalysis with the largest slope, and the RRS intensity enhanced irregularly with nanocatalyst increasing in the glycol system. Fuc was selected as reducing agents for the reduction reaction of HAuCl4. In the absence of nanocatalyst, the reaction between Fuc and HAuCl4, that is, Au(III) was very slow, owing to the slow two steps of Au. When nanocatalst CD was added, the reactants of Fuc and Au(III) adsorbed on its surface. The adsorption and nanosurface electron accelerated reactions. The main reactions were as follows,
(1)
Fuc + A u
I I I
→
slow
A u
I
(2)
Fuc + A u
( I )
→
slow
A u
(3)
Fuc + A u
( I I I )
→
CD , f a s t
A u
( I I I )
(4)
Fuc + A u
( I )
→
CD , f a s t
A u
(5)
n A u → A u N P

The nanocatalysis was heterogeneous, where the catalyst and the reactants or products are in different phases. They are located in two different phases in the Fuc-HAuCl4 system, and with AuNPs. In heterogeneous catalytic systems, the rate was related to the uncovered surface coverage of nanocatalyst. The surface coverage follows the Langmuir equation assuming that the carbon quantum dots are homogeneous surfaces.

In this case, a cycle of molecular adsorption, reaction, and desorption occurs on the catalyst surface. Adsorption is an essential step in heterogeneous catalysis. Adsorption is the process by which molecules of the solution phase (adsorbate) bind to atoms on the solid surface (adsorbent). In a reaction promoted by multiphase catalysis, the catalyst is the adsorbent, and the reactants are the adsorbates. The reactant molecules collide with the catalyst in solution due to irregular motion, and the force that binds the two is the van der Waals force. The Apt binds to the catalyst by van der Waals forces in addition to partial hydrogen bonding (the surface of the quantum dot is rich in functional groups), and the Apt can only detach from the catalyst and the surface when it encounters an analyte with which it is more tightly bound. The bond energy of hydrogen bonding is 20–40 kJ/mol, which is approximately equal to the kinetic energy of the thermal motion of the molecule at room temperature. Chemisorption occurs when a molecule is close enough to the surface atoms so that their electron clouds overlap. The typical energy range for chemisorption is 80–400 kJ/mol. Both adsorptions are weakly bound due to the absence of covalent bond formation. When the Apt is added to the system, the Apt and the reactants form an adsorption competition on the catalyst surface. In non-homogeneous catalysis, reactant molecules diffuse from the liquid phase to adsorb to the catalyst surface. However, the adsorption sites are not always active catalyst sites, so the reactant molecules must migrate across the surface to the active sites. The product molecules then desorb from the surface and diffuse away. The catalytic activity of CDN will be reduced. The adsorption and desorption of Apts on the catalyst surface affect the adsorption process of reactants and catalysts, and mediate the regulation of catalytic effect. CDN is a more efficient catalyst as a nanoparticle with high electron density ascribing to small diameter, large specific surface area, various unsaturated bonds and electron-giving groups on the surface, and more active sites for reactant adsorption. Fuc and HAuCl4 can undergo redox reaction, in which Au(III) is reduced to Au0, and the ketone group of Fuc is converted into aldehyde group by enolization. CDN with more surface electrons can fully contact with the reactants to accelerate the redox electron transfer of HAuCl4-Fuc to generate more AuNPs.

The reaction conditions of the Pb2+-Apt-CDN-Fuc-HAuCl4-MG analytical system were optimized according to the experimental method of SERS. It was found that the ΔI of the system reached the maximum at the reaction temperature of 75 °C, the reaction time of 20 min, CDN concentration of 11.25 μg/mL, Apt concentration of 0.10 μmol/L, HAuCl4 concentration of 0.25 mmol/L, Fuc concentration of 5.0 mmol/L and MG concentration of 0.10 μmoL/L (Fig. S4A-G). Examining the effect of reaction temperature, the system did not react when it was 50–60 °C, and CDN could catalyze the Fuc-HAuCl4 reaction when it was greater than 60 °C. However, the blank value I0 also increased with the temperature increase, resulting in a decreasing trend of ΔI when the reaction temperature was greater than 75 °C. Examining the effect of reaction time, ΔI reached the maximum when the reaction time was more significant than 20 min, and then tended to be stable. Examining the effect of Apt concentration. Apt was adsorbed on the surface of CDN to regulate the catalytic effect. Suppose the active site on the CDN surface is fully occupied. In that case, increasing the amount of Apt does not change in the catalytic effect, and the ΔI tends to be stable, which can also be seen from the previous Apt-Pb and Apt-As fluorescence inhibition experiments. Examining the effects of CDN, HAuCl4, and Fuc concentration, the amount of AuNPs generated by the Fuc- HAuCl4 reaction was proportional to that of CDN, but when a large amount of generated AuNPs aggregated and precipitated prematurely instead led to a decrease in I value. In addition, 1.0 mol/L NaCl solution was used as an AuNPs aggregation agent with a final concentration of 50 mmol/L.

According to the experimental method, each concentration was measured three times in parallel, and the average value was taken to draw the working curve. The SERS and RRS working curves of Pb2+-Apt-CDN and AsO2−-Apt-CDN systems were plotted. In the SERS detection method, the linear equation is ΔI1617cm-1 = 461.5C + 699.3 in the Pb2+ concentration range of 0.5–120 nmol/L, the coefficient is 0.9991, and the DL is 0.11 nmol/L. In the RRS detection method, Pb2+ concentration in the range of 50–400 nmol/L, the linear equation is ΔI375 nm = 11.4C + 24.7, the coefficient is 0.9914, and the DL is 15.32 nmol/L (Table 1 ).

In the SERS detection method, the concentration of AsO2 − in the range of 1–80 nmol / L, the linear equation of AsO2 − concentration and SERS intensity ΔI1617cm-1 is ΔI1617cm-1 = 206.4C + 793.7, the goodness of fit is 0.9849, and the DL is 0.34 nmol / L. In the RRS detection method, the concentration of AsO2 − in the range of 50–350 nmol / L, the linear equation of AsO2 −concentration and RRS intensity ΔI375nm is ΔI375nm = 4.1C + 103.9, the goodness of fit is 0.9791, and the DL is 20.24 nmol / L (Table S2). For the Pb2 +/AsO2 −Apt-CDN-Fuc-HAuCl4-MG two analysis systems, the detection of Pb2+ is more sensitive than the AsO2 −.

The interference of common ions was investigated at a Pb2 + concentration of 10 nmol/L with 10 μg/mL CDN as a catalyst. When the allowable multiple is 100 times, the relative error is less than 10 %. The interference of common substances on the determination is shown in Table S3, indicating no interference. This method has good selectivity.

In this experiment, three samples were collected on the spot. Sample 1 was laboratory tap water; Sample 2 was a brand of alkaline battery electrolyte, diluted 100 times with ultrapure water; Sample 3 was a brand of water-based paint, diluted 100 times by ultrasonic centrifugation to take the supernatant for use. Sample 2 and Sample 3 were diluted with ultrapure water before use. The amount of sample used for each detection was 200 μL. The SERS method measured the water samples five times without adding samples as blank liquid control. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.94−2.71 % (Table S4). The recovery rate was 98.72−101.20 % when 5.0 nmol/L Pb2+ or 50 nmol/L Pb2+ was added to the sample. This shows that the experimental method is accurate and reliable.

In this paper, the stable and highly catalytic CDN were synthesized using a microwave hydrothermal procedure, which was characterized by their simplicity and environmental friendliness. Compared to the reported methods for detecting lead ions, our SERS and RRS analytical strategy overcomes the drawback of the lack of Raman activity of Pb2+ by utilizing the catalytic generation of AuNPs as SERS substrates through the Fuc-HAuCl4-CDN reaction. The catalytic activity of CDN was modulated by Apt non-covalently bound to the CDN, eliminating the need to construct an Apt molecular probe. The binding of Apt with CDs and target molecules does not affect the SERS substrate and probe, thereby avoiding the reduction of the SERS detection sensitivity. By combining the empathetic catalytic amplification reaction of carbon dots with the high selectivity of the Apt and SERS and RRS scattering spectroscopic techniques, a new method for determining lead ions and arsenite was established, which exhibits advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, and high precision. Through testing on real samples, our method is simple, fast, and has good practical value.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22266013, 22264005), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA035073) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Education Department (No. 2022KY0509).
The data that support the findings of this study are available in the supporting information of this article.

Hui Li: Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Caina Jiang: Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xue He: Software, Formal analysis. Chongning Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Zhiliang Jiang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123146.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
